Healthcare delivery market projected to grow from US$82B (2025) to US$110B (2028E), 11% CAGR; drivers: rising incomes, insurance penetration, elevated lifestyle diseases, government transitioning from active provider to payer, ~60% of population in Tier-2+ cities driving hospital capacity expansion (listed chains plan ~31% of next-5-yr bed additions there); key themes: tertiary-care consolidation, scaled single-specialty platforms, integrated diagnostics, medical-device export potential
TMT topped Q3 2025 deal volume with 119 transactions (up from 78 in Q2 2025 and 98 in Q3 2024), reflecting sustained investor interest in digital and tech-driven businesses

India is the world's 4th-largest renewable energy market; US$4B+ FDI in the past year; 220GW installed capacity (45% of energy mix); 60+ marquee global investors; shift toward 'RE 2.0'; sector positioned for US$500B-1T investment over the next 5 years; Power sector led H1 2025 M&A deal value at US$8.5B, ~80% from renewable energy (up from US$3.2B in H1 2024)

Consumer products and retail led H1 2025 deal volume with 205 transactions (vs 218 in H1 2024, 203 in H2 2024), reflecting stable, continued investor interest in consumer-facing businesses despite macro headwinds

Deal activity rebounded sharply in 2024: 857 deals worth US$32.2B disclosed value (vs 722 deals / US$7.0B in 2023); IT spend growth held flat at 4-5%; weak public markets plus an easing rate cycle created attractive PE take-private entry points (deal value spiked 2x, ~10 large US$500m+ deals announced); PE-led middle-market consolidation reached 4-year highs; GenAI acted as a net positive catalyst for differentiated IP-led cloud-ecosystem assets

YTD 2024 PE firms accounted for 85% of total technology-services deal value (~US$15B) vs 50% (US$4B) in CY23; Healthcare & Life Sciences (HLS) IT-services sub-vertical grew at 12.9% CAGR vs 6.8% for overall IT services, driven by demand for digital patient-experience capabilities, GenAI use in drug development/commercialization, and Real World Data (RWD) analytics

2022 saw record deal activity (947 deals, highest in 5 years) after 8 consecutive quarters of hypergrowth, before a slowdown began in late 2022 / Q1 2023; PE investors drove 15 of 23 large (US$500m+) deals; top strategic imperatives were cloud value-chain technology and vertical specialists (BFSI & Life Sciences); mega-deals included the ~US$7B LTI-Mindtree merger and the ~US$4B CloudMed-R1 RCM (BPM) deal

PE exits rose to 55+ in 2022 (vs 43 in 2021, 24 in 2020); investment horizons shortened from a typical 5-7 years to under 3 years as PE investors sought to capture rich private-market valuations; roughly one-third of exits were PE recapitalizations

ArcelorMittal-Essar Steel (USD7.25bn) was the largest deal of 2019, an IBC-driven consolidation; top 10 M&A deals accounted for 58% of total M&A deal values while only 2% of volumes. Second IBC-driven deal Patanjali-Ruchi Soya also featured in the top-10.

M&A activity moderate; largest M&A deal was L&T's 20% stake purchase in Mindtree (USD476m). PE top deals concentrated in cloud technology, data analytics/AI and software development (Baring PE-NIIT Technologies USD381m largest).

Consolidation among travel/logistics and foodtech players (Ebix-Yatra, Hyundai/Kia-Ola, Delivery Hero-Zomato UAE unit). PE funding concentrated in fintech (Paytm) and logistics/retail-tech (Delhivery, Lenskart, Grofers, BigBasket, PolicyBazaar).

Hospitals sector saw major consolidation via Radiant Life Care-Max Healthcare merger (USD1.02bn). Outbound and inbound generics/API deals prominent (Dr Reddy's-Wockhardt generics business, Novavax-Serum Institute).

Nippon Life-Reliance Nippon Life AMC (USD860m, 43% stake) was the largest deal. PE activity strong in insurance and NBFC (Warburg Pincus/Premji Invest-SBI General Insurance, Blackstone-Aadhaar Housing Finance).

Patanjali's IBC acquisition of Ruchi Soya (USD613m) led the sector. Amazon made two significant minority-stake inbound deals (Witzig Advisory Services, Future Coupons) as it built its position in Future Group ecosystem.

2020 recorded 1,313 deals worth USD77.7bn (M&A USD37.5bn across 358 deals, PE USD40.2bn across 953 deals). A near-total absence of insolvency/IBC-driven deals was noted due to the moratorium on new insolvency admissions during COVID-19. Reliance-led telecom/retail megadeals (Jio Platforms stake sales to Facebook, Google and a consortium of PE investors; Reliance Retail-Future Group) dominated deal values.

Real estate saw a near-standstill in H1 2020 followed by a pickup, highlighted by the successful Mindspace REIT listing and Blackstone's acquisition of Prestige's commercial/retail/hotel portfolio (USD1.24bn) and Embassy Office Parks REIT-Embassy TechVillage merger (USD1.32bn).

Clarivate Analytics' USD950m acquisition of Piramal's Decision Resources Group was the standout inbound M&A deal. PE activity concentrated in SaaS/software (Thoma Bravo-Majesco USD421m, Carlyle-Nxtra Data USD235m).

Hospital consolidation continued (Manipal-Columbia Asia). Generics business carve-outs were prominent (Dr Reddy's-Wockhardt). PE backed API/CDMO and biologics scale-up (New Mountain Capital-Aurobindo's Natrol, Carlyle-Piramal Pharma, Goldman Sachs-Biocon Biologics).

YES Bank's rescue recapitalisation by a consortium of Indian banks (SBI, HDFC, ICICI, Axis, Kotak and others, USD1.39bn) was the year's largest banking deal amid COVID-19-driven stress. PE investors focused on capital adequacy support for NBFCs and banks (PAG-Edelweiss Wealth Management, Brookfield-IndoStar Capital Finance).

Reliance Retail's USD3.3bn acquisition of Future Group's retail, wholesale and logistics business was the marquee domestic deal, later contested by Amazon via arbitration and CCI proceedings. HUL's Horlicks India (GSK Consumer Healthcare) acquisition for USD412m also featured.

2021 was a record year for PE (USD48.2bn across 1,624 deals, the highest since 2011) even as the second COVID-19 wave hit the economy in Q2. Start-up, e-commerce and IT & ITeS drove 81% of PE deal volumes and over 67% of PE fund inflows (USD32.2bn). The year saw the birth of 33 unicorns.

Start-up M&A volumes grew 73% YoY to 122 deals worth USD857m, driven partly by the emergence of Thrasio-style roll-up start-ups (Mensa Brands, GlobalBees, 10Club, Upscalio, Evenflow, Powerhouse91, GOAT Brand Labs) which executed 28 M&A deals in the year.
Piramal Capital & Housing Finance's USD5.1bn acquisition of Dewan Housing Finance Corporation (DHFL) via the IBC process was the largest M&A deal of the year and of the financial services sector.

Byju's pursued an aggressive acquisition strategy, acquiring Aakash Educational Services (USD1bn), Great Learning (USD600m), Epic! Creations (USD500m) and Tynker (USD200m) in the same year, alongside raising multiple large funding rounds exceeding USD1.7bn cumulatively from global investors.

Prosus/PayU's USD4.7bn acquisition of BillDesk (IndiaIdeas.com) was the largest inbound digital payments deal in India's history. Wipro's USD1.45bn acquisition of Capco marked its largest ever acquisition.
Deal activity was led by diagnostics consolidation (Docon Technologies-Thyrocare, USD623m) and API/animal health scale-up (Multiples-Zydus Animal Health, USD395m PE deal).
Adani Green Energy's USD3.5bn acquisition of SB Energy India was the largest outbound-classified M&A deal, alongside Total SE taking a 20% stake in Adani Green Energy for USD2.48bn.

India Inc. recorded over 2,000 deals worth over USD127bn in 2022, a 47% increase in values YoY, driven by landmark deals (HDFC-HDFC Bank merger USD40bn, L&T-Mindtree/LTI-Mindtree merger USD17.7bn, Adani's USD10.5bn acquisition of Ambuja Cement/ACC). Domestic M&A saw record deal values of USD70.7bn across 355 deals, the highest average ticket size on record.

Start-up M&A volumes grew 73% YoY (year framed against 2021 comparison) but PE start-up funding began cooling amid tightening global liquidity in H2 2022; PE/VC deal value in new ventures still hit a record USD11bn across 1,072 deals for the year overall (per sector-spotlight figures).

BFSI saw megadeals create history: the HDFC-HDFC Bank USD40bn merger anchored the year, while fintech (NBFC/insurance) continued strong momentum with deals like Carlyle/Advent's USD1.1bn stake in Yes Bank.
Acceleration of digitisation remained the key sector theme; L&T Infotech-Mindtree's USD17.7bn merger was the standout deal of the year, alongside Baring PE Asia's USD800m investment in IGT Solutions.
Biotech firms drove M&A (Biocon's USD3.34bn outbound acquisition of Viatris' generics business) while API and healthcare drove PE investment activity (PAG/CX Partners/Samara Capital-Optimus Drugs).

Labelled 'Green Energy is the new buzz' - the sector recorded 27 deals worth USD6.5bn, driven by Shell's USD1.55bn Sprng Energy-Solenergi Power investment and JSW Neo Energy's USD1.3bn acquisition of Mytrah Energy's 1.75GW renewable portfolio; cleantech led PE deal volumes (~59% share).
Industrials witnessed the sector's largest-ever deal: Adani's USD10.5bn acquisition of Ambuja Cement and ACC from Holcim, accounting for 84% of total manufacturing sector deal values for the year.

Entertainment remained active with Viacom18's USD1.8bn fundraise from Bodhi Tree Systems and Ver Se Innovation's USD805m fundraise from a consortium including CPPIB, contributing 80% of the sector's deal value.

2023 recorded 1,641 deals worth USD65.9bn, a 21% decrease in volumes and 52% decline in values YoY, reflecting a global funding winter and cautious investor sentiment; only eight multi-billion-dollar M&A/PE deals were recorded versus 11 in 2022. Domestic M&A hit its lowest level in three years (350 deals, USD14.4bn) while inbound M&A rose 38% in volume and 178% in value (USD7.7bn) driven by two multi-billion-dollar deals.

The hospital sub-sector emerged as the top performer, surpassing life sciences/biotech in deal value; Temasek's USD2bn investment in Manipal Health Enterprises was the year's largest PE healthcare deal (34% of sector PE value). Nirma-Glenmark Life Sciences (USD689m) was the largest M&A deal in the sector.

M&A volumes were driven by consolidation in banking/NBFC segments and a rush of inbound insurance and TPA transactions as foreign players sought exposure to the Indian insurance market; overall PE activity in FinTech fell 53% YoY even as NBFC PE interest rose (37 deals vs 24 in 2022).

Technology deal-making and valuations 'took a knock' in 2023 amid global macroeconomic weakness and cost optimisation by tech buyers; no billion-dollar tech deals were recorded (versus the USD17.7bn L&T-Mindtree merger in 2022). GenAI is flagged as the next big opportunity expected to drive a 2024 rebound.

Cautious investor approach and shifting consumer preference defined the year: M&A activity fell 36% to USD3.4bn and PE investment fell 36% to USD5.2bn YoY, though e-commerce festive-season order volumes and GMV grew 18-22%, with Tier 2/3 cities playing a bigger role.

Auto components led M&A activity while electric vehicles (EVs) continued to dominate the PE segment; overall sector deal value rose 41% YoY to USD2.9bn even as deal count held flat, signalling consolidation. Ola Electric alone attracted around USD530m in PE funding across multiple 2023 tranches ahead of its planned IPO.

Start-ups faced a slowdown, with only 81 M&A transactions in 2023 versus 133 in 2022, and deal value falling to USD655m from USD1bn, reflecting cautious investor sentiment amid the prevailing global funding winter and a shift in investor focus toward unit economics and profitability over growth.
